  • Theory
  • Convection Models: Why We Use Bot, Not TARP
  • Convection Models: Why We Use Bot, Not TARP

Convection Models: Why We Use Bot, Not TARP

The problem

GreenhousesJL and EnergyPlus give different heating demands on small greenhouses (+23% on a 5×5 m Savona prototype). Diagnostic testing confirmed the gap comes from different convection correlations, not from radiation, T_sky, or solar models.

Greenhouse-specific correlations (what we use)

Exterior: Bot (1983)

\[h_{ext} = \begin{cases} (2.8 + 1.2 \cdot u) / \cos\phi & u < 4 \text{ m/s} \\ 2.5 \cdot u^{0.8} / \cos\phi & u \geq 4 \text{ m/s} \end{cases}\]

Bot experimentally characterized heat transfer on saw-tooth greenhouse roof surfaces at Wageningen University. The correlation accounts for the smooth glass surface, the inclined geometry (via 1/cos(φ)), and the open-field wind exposure typical of greenhouses.

Reference: Bot, G.P.A. (1983). Greenhouse climate: from physical processes to a dynamic model. PhD thesis, Wageningen University.

Interior: Balemans (1989)

\[h_{in} = \begin{cases} 1.7 \cdot |\Delta T|^{0.33} \cdot \cos\phi^{-0.66} & \text{inclined cover} \\ 1.7 \cdot |\Delta T|^{0.33} & \text{floor, upward} \\ 1.3 \cdot |\Delta T|^{0.25} & \text{floor, downward} \end{cases}\]

Balemans measured free convection inside greenhouses, accounting for the specific geometry (large floor area, inclined cover, low ceiling, still air). The bidirectional floor model captures the day/night reversal (floor warmer by day, cooler at night).

Reference: Balemans, L. (1989). Assessment of criteria for energetic effectiveness of greenhouse screens. PhD thesis, Agricultural University, Ghent.

Building-oriented correlations (what EnergyPlus uses)

TARP (Thermal Analysis Research Program)

\[h_{ext} = h_{natural} + h_{forced}\]

TARP was developed for office buildings with brick/concrete walls in urban environments. Its wind exposure model assumes partial shielding by neighboring buildings. Its surface roughness assumes masonry, not smooth glass.

DOE-2

\[h_{ext} = \sqrt{h_{forced}^2 + h_{natural}^2}\]

The quadratic combination gives a smoother transition between forced and natural regimes but was also calibrated on building surfaces.

Why they give different results

PropertyBot 1983 (greenhouses)TARP/DOE-2 (buildings)
Calibrated onGlass saw-tooth roof, WageningenBrick/concrete, office buildings
Surface typeSmooth, inclined glassRough, vertical masonry
Wind exposureOpen fieldUrban, partially shielded
Tilt correctionExplicit 1/cos(φ)Generic orientation factors
Typical h at 3 m/s6.4 W/(m²K) for cover10–15 W/(m²K) for vertical wall

For a standard Venlo (14 000 m², walls < 3% of envelope), the convection model barely matters because the roof dominates. Both use Bot for the roof.

For a small greenhouse like Savona (5×5 m, walls = 60%), the wall convection model matters a lot. Bot was not specifically calibrated for vertical glass walls (it was developed for the inclined saw-tooth roof). TARP was calibrated for vertical surfaces but not for glass.

Neither is "wrong" — they're calibrated for different geometries.

Who else uses Bot?

Bot 1983 is the de facto standard in greenhouse climate modelling:

  • Vanthoor et al. (2011): the Modelica Greenhouses library we ported
  • GreenLight (Katzin et al. 2020): Wageningen open-source greenhouse model
  • de Zwart (1996): Wageningen energy-saving analysis model
  • Stanghellini (1987): foundational greenhouse transpiration model

All use Bot for exterior and Balemans for interior convection.

Impact on cross-validation

GreenhouseWall fractionBot vs TARP impact
Venlo 14 000 m²< 3%Negligible (< 6% Q_heat gap)
Medium 1 000 m²~10%Small (~2–3°C T_air)
Savona 5×5 m60%Significant (+23% Q_heat)

The +23% Savona gap is a structural difference between greenhouse-calibrated and building-calibrated convection, not a model error. It confirms that greenhouse-specific correlations are necessary and that generic building tools (EnergyPlus) should be used with caution for small greenhouse geometries.
